actions.thought: I hold AhKs (Ace-King offsuit), which is a premium hand, especially in a Heads-Up configuration where it dominates the vast majority of the opponent's range. Being on the Button (SB), I have the advantage of position post-flop. Limping is too passive and allows the Big Blind to realize equity for free. My goal is to build the pot for value and seize the initiative. A standard opening raise size is between 2x and 2.5x the big blind. I will raise to 250 (2.5bb) to extract value from weaker Ax/Kx hands and pairs while balancing my opening range.

In a heads-up situation, K3s is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a standard 2.5x button open. The hand has good high-card value and flush potential. Calling allows me to see the flop and play a pot in position relative to the blinds (though out of position post-flop in heads-up) without risking too many chips on the first hand of the tournament.

I have flopped top pair with King-Trey. While my kicker is weak, top pair in a heads-up pot is a very strong hand. The board is somewhat coordinated with a flush draw and some straight possibilities. As the player out of position, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. Checking allows me to evaluate their bet size or potentially see a free turn if they check back, while also protecting my checking range.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AhKs) with the second-nut flush draw on a Qs 7s Kh board. This is an exceptionally strong hand. The board texture is wet with straight and flush draws available, so providing a free card by checking is risky. I want to build the pot for value against Queen-x hands, straight draws (JT, T9), and inferior flush draws. While I block some Kx and flush draws, there are enough worse hands that can call. A continuation bet of roughly 40% of the pot (200 chips) strikes a good balance: it extracts value and charges draws, but isn't so large that it folds out the weaker parts of the opponent's range like middle pair.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a board with several draws. The opponent's bet of 200 into 500 is a standard continuation bet. Folding is out of the question with top pair, but raising might lead to getting value-owned by better Kings or two-pair combinations. Calling allows me to control the pot size and see how the turn develops while keeping the opponent's bluffs and weaker hands in the pot.

The turn brings the 4 of spades, completing the flush draw that was present on the flop. My top pair with a weak kicker (K3) has decreased in relative value. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor on a card that potentially improves their range or completes draws is generally a mistake. I will check to see how the opponent reacts. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a showdown more cheaply or evaluate their betting patterns on this board texture.

The turn brings the 4s, putting three spades on the board (Qs 7s 4s). I hold AhKs, meaning I have Top Pair Top Kicker and the second-nut flush draw (King-high flush draw). The arrival of the third spade is a scare card that completes possible flushes for the opponent if they floated the flop with suited connectors or suited gappers. Betting here puts me in a difficult spot if I face a check-raise, as I would likely have to fold my equity or commit heavily with a marginal made hand. Checking back is the superior option for several reasons: 1) Pot control with a one-pair hand. 2) I realize my equity for free, trying to hit the flush on the river. 3) I can induce bluffs from the opponent on the river or comfortably call a reasonable bet. 4) I avoid folding out worse hands (like KQ, KJ, Qx) that might fold to a double barrel on this scary board.

The river completes a four-flush on the board with the 3 of spades. While I have two pair (Kings and Threes), the presence of four spades makes my hand extremely vulnerable to any single spade in the opponent's hole cards. Since I do not hold a spade myself, I cannot represent the flush effectively, and I am unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet. Pro checked back the turn, which could indicate a lack of a spade at that point, but the 3s on the river is a dangerous card regardless. Checking is the most sensible play to minimize losses and see a showdown if the opponent also checks.

On the river, the board reads Qs 7s Kh 4s 3s, putting four spades on the board. I hold the King of Spades (Ks), giving me the second-nut flush. The only hand that beats me is the Ace of Spades. Since I checked back the turn, my hand is under-represented, and the opponent likely does not put me on a strong flush. The opponent checked to me, which suggests they do not hold the Ace of Spades (as they would likely bet the nuts for value given my turn check). There are many worse spades (Js, Ts, 9s, etc.) in the Big Blind's defending range that will call a value bet. Checking back misses value from these hands. I will bet half the pot (450) to extract value from inferior flushes. If I face a check-raise, I can confidently fold, assuming the opponent holds the Ace of Spades.

On a board with four spades, my two pair (Kings and Threes) is essentially a bluff-catcher since any single spade in the opponent's hand completes a flush. Pro's check on the turn suggests they might not have had a flush when the third spade appeared, although they could be pot-controlling or waiting for the river. The river bet of 450 into 900 gives me 4:1 pot odds, meaning I only need to be right 20% of the time to break even. Given that this is a heads-up match and the board texture is highly conducive to bluffing or thin value-betting, I will call to see the showdown and gain information on Pro's playstyle in the first hand of the tournament.

Holding Ace-high on the button in heads-up play, I have a strong enough hand to open for a raise. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take control of the hand and gain information on the opponent's range while maintaining a healthy stack-to-pot ratio.

I hold 8h 2c in the Big Blind, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em. My opponent on the Button has raised to 300 (3bb). The pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), requiring roughly 33% equity to break even. However, 82o has very poor playability and equity, even against a wide Button opening range. Additionally, I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. Defending here is significantly negative EV. Therefore, the correct decision is to fold.

I am on the Button (SB) with J8o. In Heads-Up play, this hand is within the top 55% of hands and has roughly 51% equity against a random hand. While it is a marginal holding, folding the button too frequently is exploitable. Given the stack depth (approx 100BB) and the fact that we are early in the match, establishing an aggressive baseline is important. I will make a standard min-raise to 200 to apply pressure to the Big Blind and utilize my positional advantage post-flop. If the opponent (Flash) is sticky as suggested by Hand #1, I will be cautious post-flop if I don't improve.

I am holding 9d 2c in the Big Blind. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, 92o is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly out of position. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

In a heads-up situation, 8-6 offsuit is a reasonable hand to open for a raise from the button (Small Blind). I want to apply pressure and take control of the pot early. A 3x raise (300 total) is a standard opening size that forces the Big Blind to make a decision.

Opponent has raised to 3bb (300) from the button. In heads-up play, K3 suited is a standard defense due to its high card value and flush potential, even against a slightly larger 3x sizing. The pot odds are 2:1, and K3s has roughly 45-50% equity against a typical wide button opening range. Defending here prevents over-folding the Big Blind.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a weak kicker on a very wet, monotone board (Ks Qs 7s). I do not hold a spade, which makes my hand vulnerable to flushes, and draws. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard line is to check to the raiser. Leading out (donk betting) would fold out worse hands and isolate me against better hands or strong draws. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's action, looking to keep the pot smaller given my kicker issues and the dangerous board texture.

I have 8-high with no spades on a monochrome King-High board. This texture is quite coordinated and likely interacts with the Big Blind's calling range. Since I have zero equity and no spade in my hand to use as a bluff blocker, betting is risky. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and keep the pot small while playing in position.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (Kc3c) on a board of [Ks Qs 7s 8h]. The flop was checked through. The opponent's flop check-back suggests they likely do not have a made flush or a very strong hand that needs protection, effectively capping their range to marginal made hands (like Qx, weak Kx) or air/weak draws. While I have the best hand frequently here, my kicker is poor and I lack a spade re-draw. Betting (probing) folds out their air and isolates me against better Kings or slow-played monsters. Checking allows me to keep the pot small, induce bluffs from their air, or catch a bluff on the river. I intend to call a reasonable bet since the flop check makes a strong flush unlikely.

I have improved to a pair of eights on the turn. While the board is coordinated with three spades and two high cards (King and Queen), the Big Blind has checked to me twice. My pair of eights has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value given the board texture. Checking back allows me to reach the river for free and potentially win at showdown or evaluate the final board state without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a weak kicker (3) on a board of [Ks Qs 7s 8h Jc]. The action went check-check on both the flop and the turn. The opponent's passivity suggests they do not have a strong flush, set, or strong top pair, capping their range to marginal made hands (like Qx, weak pairs) or missed draws. The river Jack is a scare card that completes straights (T9, AT) and improves broadway hands (KJ, QJ) that might have checked back. Betting here is thin value; I likely only get called by better hands (better Kings, two pair, straights) while folding out worse hands I'm currently beating. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply against their weaker range (Qx, 8x, underpairs) or induce a bluff from missed draws, though bluffs are less likely after two checks. My hand is a classic bluff-catcher/showdown hand in this spot.

I have a pair of eights on a board with three spades and several high cards (King, Queen, Jack). The opponent has checked to me on every street including the river. While my pair of eights is not a powerhouse, it has significant showdown value in a pot that has remained small. Betting for value is unlikely to be called by worse hands, and betting as a bluff is unnecessary since I may already have the best hand. Checking back allows me to take the hand to showdown and win the pot if the opponent was holding a weaker hand or a missed draw.

In Heads-Up play, A7o is a strong starting hand (top ~30%), well above the median. Being on the Button (Small Blind), I have the advantage of position post-flop. I want to play this hand aggressively to extract value or take down the pot pre-flop. A min-raise to 200 is a standard and efficient size that puts pressure on the Big Blind while keeping the pot manageable if called. This matches my action in Hand #3 where I successfully stole the blinds.

I am in the Big Blind holding Seven of Clubs and Deuce of Hearts (72o), which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Pro, on the Button, has raised to 200. While I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, 72o is extremely difficult to play profitably out of position. It lacks both straight and flush potential and will rarely win at showdown without significant improvement. Against a standard Button raising range, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand even in heads-up play. While I have position, the hand has poor post-flop playability and equity. Pro has shown a willingness to defend and play pots. Given the stack sizes and the weakness of my hand, I will elect to fold and wait for a better opportunity to engage.

In Heads-Up play, J4o is a very weak hand (bottom ~15% of hands). While I have the Button and the opponent has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop raises in the Big Blind (Hands 3 and 5), previous hands (Hand 1 and 4) indicate the opponent is somewhat of a 'calling station' post-flop, calling down with marginal hands. Raising with trash against a calling station is risky because bluffing post-flop will be difficult if they don't fold. Therefore, the most prudent play with this specific holding is to fold and wait for a hand with better playability.

I am holding pocket nines, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take advantage of my positional advantage on the button. This allows me to define the hand early and put pressure on the big blind.

I hold Pocket Eights (8s 8c) in the Big Blind. My opponent (Button/SB) has raised to 300 (3bb). With a stack depth of over 80 big blinds, Pocket Eights is a strong hand but plays somewhat trickily out of position in a bloated pot if I 3-bet and get called or 4-bet. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide, keeps the pot manageable, and gives me excellent implied odds to hit a set or bluff-catch on low-textured boards. The price to call is 200 chips into a pot that will be 600, offering decent direct odds. I choose to defend my blind by flat calling.

I hold Pocket Eights on a T-6-2 rainbow board. This is a fairly dry texture. While I have an underpair to the top card (Ten), my hand is still reasonably strong relative to the opponent's wide button opening range. Leading out (donk betting) usually accomplishes little here, as it folds out bluffs and isolates me against better hands like Tx or higher pairs. The standard and most profitable line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If they bet, I can comfortably call (check-call) given the dryness of the board and my showdown value. If they check back, I remain ahead of their ace-highs and smaller pairs.

I have pocket nines on a dry board of Ts-2h-6d. While the Ten is an overcard to my pair, I am likely ahead of a significant portion of Pro's calling range, including lower pairs (2x, 6x, 77, 88) and high-card hands. Since Pro checked, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) to extract value and protect my hand from overcards on the turn.

I hold Pocket Eights on a dry T-6-2 rainbow board. My opponent, the pre-flop aggressor on the Button, has continued with a half-pot bet (300 into 600). This texture connects somewhat with a Button opening range (Tx, sets), but it is also a very standard board for an opponent to c-bet with their entire range, including overcards (AK, AQ) and total air. My hand beats all their bluffs and lower pairs (44, 55, 33). Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me (Tx, overpairs) while folding out the bluffs I am currently ahead of. Folding is too weak given the width of a Button range and the lack of draws on the board. The most profitable play is to call (check-call) to keep their range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold Pocket Eights on a T-6-2-4 board, giving me a middle pair. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who c-bet the flop, my primary goal is pot control and bluff-catching. Leading out (donk betting) is suboptimal as it likely isolates me against better hands (Tx, overpairs) while folding out the opponent's air and weaker holdings that I am currently beating. The turn card brings a second diamond, opening a flush draw, but is otherwise relatively safe. I will check to the aggressor to see if they continue their aggression or take a free card, keeping their range wide.

I have a solid middle pair with pocket nines, but the Ten on the board is an overcard that beats me. Since Pro called my flop continuation bet and has now checked the turn, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise, and see the river for free. This strategy preserves my stack while keeping me in a good position to reach showdown or react to Pro's final action.

I hold Pocket Eights on a T-6-2-4-K board. After calling the flop c-bet and watching the opponent check back the turn, the pot is 1200. The river King is an overcard that connects with many hands in the opponent's range that might check the turn (e.g., KQ, KJ, KT). Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (like Ace-high or lower pairs) as they will fold, and better hands (Tx, Kx) will call. Checking is the best option to realize my showdown value against his bluffs and weaker pairs, or to evaluate if he decides to bluff at the scary river card.

I have pocket nines, which is currently a middle pair on a board with two overcards (Ten and King). After the turn went check-check, Pro has checked the river as well. While I might be ahead of smaller pairs or missed hands, there is very little value in betting as better hands (like a Ten or a King) will call, and worse hands will likely fold. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot without risking more chips.

I am holding 5c 3d on the Button (SB) in a Heads-Up match. This is 5-3 offsuit, which is at the very bottom of my range. Although I have position and previous hands suggest the opponent folds to button raises occasionally (Hand 3 and 5), the sample size is small. The hand has very poor playability and equity if called. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand pre-flop to avoid entering a pot with a significant disadvantage.

QTo is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, significantly ahead of a random range. I will raise to 300, which has been my standard opening size, to build the pot and maintain initiative from the button.

I am in the Big Blind with J9 suited facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash). J9s is a strong defending hand in heads-up play due to its playability and flush/straight potential. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (need to call 200 to win a pot that will be 600), requiring 33% equity. J9s has significantly more equity than that against a standard wide button opening range. I will call to see a flop rather than 3-betting, which keeps the opponent's range wide and avoids bloating the pot while out of position.

I hold Jc9c on a Qs 6s 3h board. I have missed the flop completely, holding only Jack-high with no immediate straight or flush draws (as the board flush draw is Spades and I hold Clubs). The Queen-high texture favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes AQ, KQ, and pocket pairs. Donk betting here is ineffective as I have little equity if called. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold given my lack of connection to the board. If they check back, I get to see a free Turn.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a board of Q-6-3. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play, likely ahead of my opponent's range. Pro checked, allowing me to continue my aggression after being the pre-flop raiser. I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is 50% of the pot, to extract value from weaker pairs like 6x or 3x, as well as to charge any spade flush draws for their equity.

I hold Jc9c on a Qs 6s 3h flop. I checked to the pre-flop raiser, who has now bet 300 (half pot). I have no pair, no flush draw (my suit is clubs, board is spades), and no straight draw. While I have two overcards to the 6 and 3, the Queen connects well with the opponent's raising range. Even against a bluff, I am likely behind Ace-high or King-high. Without a strong draw or backdoor equity, calling out of position is unprofitable. The pot odds of 3:1 are not sufficient to justify chasing with Jack-high.

I am on the Button with Q4o. This is a very weak hand, statistically below average even for Heads-Up play. While position is an advantage, Q4o has poor playability and is easily dominated. I have previously folded hands like J4o and 53o in this position. Although my opponent has folded to some of my previous raises, the risk of getting called and playing a large pot with a weak hand outweighs the potential blind steal reward here. Folding is the standard disciplined play.

95o is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. While I am on the button, Pro has shown some willingness to defend their big blind or play post-flop in previous hands (like Hands 4, 8, and 10). I will fold this hand and look for better opportunities to exploit Pro's tendencies.

I hold AQo, a premium hand in heads-up play. Being on the button (SB), I want to play this hand for value and utilize my position. In Hand #1, I raised to 250 with AKo, while I raised to 200 with weaker hands in subsequent pots. To extract maximum value and potentially disguise my hand strength by mimicking the Hand #1 line (or simply building a bigger pot with a top-tier hand), I will raise to 250. This size charges the Big Blind to see a flop while giving me the initiative.

In this heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind with 9h 7d. Pro has raised to 250 from the button. The pot is currently 350, and it costs me 150 more to call, giving me pot odds of 30%. 97 offsuit is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a button open, as it has sufficient equity against a wide opening range. I will call to see the flop.

On this flop of 5c 9c 3d, I have top pair with a 7 kicker. This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot against a button raiser. However, standard poker strategy in the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out into the raiser often clarifies their hand too early and can force me into a difficult spot if they raise. By checking, I allow 'Pro' to continuation bet with their wider range of hands, including bluffs and weaker pairs, which I can then comfortably call to see the turn.

The board is 5c 9c 3d. This low, connected texture with a flush draw hits the Big Blind's defending range reasonably well (sets, two pairs, straight draws, flush draws). As the pre-flop aggressor with AQo (no clubs), I have two overcards and reasonable showdown value, but I am vulnerable to check-raises. Betting folds out hands I am already beating (like KJ, QJ) but isolates me against hands that beat me or have strong draws. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity by seeing a free turn, and potentially induce bluffs from the opponent on later streets. It also protects my checking range. Thus, checking is the prudent play.

On the turn 8c, the board now shows 5c 9c 3d 8c. I still have top pair with my 9, but the 8c completes a potential flush and some straight draws. Pro checked back on the flop, which suggests a range of mediocre made hands, high-card hands like AK/AQ that missed, or perhaps a slow-played monster. By checking again, I am controlling the pot size and allowing Pro to bluff if they have missed, while also avoiding a situation where I bet and get raised by a flush or a better made hand. I intend to call a reasonably sized bet.

The turn card is the 8c, making the board 5c 9c 3d 8c. This is a very coordinated board with three clubs and connected low cards (6-7 makes a straight). This texture favors the Big Blind's calling range significantly more than my pre-flop raising range, as they can have many flushes, straights, and two-pair combinations. Holding Ace-Queen high with no club, I have showdown value but cannot confidently bet for value. Betting would likely only fold out hands I am already beating, while getting called or raised by hands that crush me. Checking back controls the pot size and allows me to see the river for free, potentially hitting a pair or winning at showdown with Ace-high.

On the river 4d, the board is 5c 9c 3d 8c 4d. I have top pair with a 7 kicker. Pro has checked back on both the flop and the turn, which heavily caps their range to mediocre showdown value hands (like small pairs, A-high, or a weak 8) or total misses. While my 9 is likely the best hand, my kicker is weak, and Pro could easily be checking back a better 9 (like J9 or T9). By checking, I ensure I reach showdown without risking a raise, and I give Pro the opportunity to bluff with any air they might be holding. Given the three clubs on the board, a bet from me might also fold out the very hands I want to get value from, while a check-call or check-check is a safer way to realize my equity.

The board runout of 5c 9c 3d 8c 4d is extremely wet and connected, interacting heavily with a Big Blind defending range. There are three clubs, a four-card straight on the board (with 6-7 making a straight), and no overcards to the 9. After the action checked through on the flop and turn, the opponent checked the river. While my Ace-Queen high has decent showdown value against missed broadway draws or lower Ace-highs, it loses to any pair. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a pair (given the pot size is small and my line looks like I gave up or have showdown value), and betting would only fold out hands I am already beating. Thus, checking back to realize my showdown value is the highest EV play, avoiding getting check-raised or calling a bet when behind.

I am holding 6-3 offsuit on the button. This is one of the weakest hands in the game. While I should be playing a high percentage of hands from the button in a heads-up match, 6-3 offsuit is bottom-tier and generally not worth the investment against a competent opponent who has shown willingness to defend and play post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

I hold As9s on the Button (SB), which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My strategy is to raise to build the pot and seize the initiative while in position. I have previously opened to both 200 and 250 with various hand strengths. A min-raise to 200 is sufficient to deny free equity to the Big Blind while keeping the pot manageable relative to stack depths (100bb). If the opponent 3-bets, I am comfortable calling in position.

I am in the Big Blind and Pro has min-raised to 200. I have 8h 5c, which is a weak hand, but given the pot odds are 3:1 (calling 100 to win a pot that will be 400), defending the big blind in a heads-up match is generally standard against a small raise. I will call and see a flop, but plan to play cautiously if I do not hit the board strongly.

I have 8-high on a Jack-high board with no real draws. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. The standard play here is to check and see how the opponent reacts, as I have no reason to lead out with this hand.

I hold Middle Pair with a Top Kicker (Pair of Nines, Ace Kicker) on a board of 9c-Jd-2c. While I have good showdown value, the Jack is a scare card that connects well with the Big Blind's defending range. Betting here risks isolating myself against a Jack (which beats me) or getting check-raised by strong draws. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets from missed draws or worse hands. It also protects my checking range. I plan to call a bet on safe turns or bet for value if checked to again.

I am out of position with 8-high and a weak flush draw (5c) on a board that now has three clubs. The flop went check-check, suggesting Pro might not have a strong hand, but leading into them now with absolute air is risky. I will check to see what Pro does. If they bet, I will likely fold as I have no showdown value and minimal equity.

I hold middle pair (Nines) with top kicker on a board that has become significantly wetter with the arrival of the 6c, making three clubs on the board. The opponent has checked twice, signaling weakness or a draw, but betting here yields little value as worse hands likely fold and better hands (flush, Jack, two pair) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to pot control, realize my equity, and reassess on the river, aiming to get to showdown cheaply with a hand that has decent showdown value but cannot withstand significant aggression.

The river is an Ace of Hearts, and the board remains relatively dry with three clubs (9c, 2c, 6c). I have 8-high and zero showdown value. Both the flop and turn went check-check, suggesting Pro likely has a marginal hand or was looking for a cheap showdown. While the Ace is a scare card I could potentially bluff, Pro's range still contains many hands that beat 8-high even if they don't have an Ace. Since I have no chance of winning at showdown, I will check. If Pro bets, I will fold, and if Pro checks back, I simply lose a small pot.

The river card is the Ace of Hearts, improving my hand to Top Two Pair (Aces and Nines). The opponent has checked to me on the Flop, Turn, and now the River. This passive line suggests a capped range, likely containing marginal made hands like a Jack (second pair), a weak Ace (rivered top pair), or a missed straight draw. While a flush is possible given the three clubs on board, the opponent's failure to bet the river reduces this likelihood. My previous checks have under-represented my hand strength. I believe I can extract value from worse hands such as Jx or weak Ax that might call a reasonable bet. I will bet 250 (roughly 60% pot) to target these hands.

I have 8-high with no showdown value. The board has an Ace and three clubs. Pro checked the flop and turn but bet the river when the Ace landed. Whether Pro has the Ace, a Jack, a flush, or is bluffing, my 8-high cannot win the hand by calling. Folding is the only sensible option as I have no equity.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand even in heads-up play. Given the tournament structure and the fact that I want to preserve my stack for better opportunities, I will fold this hand. I have been alternating between raising and folding from the button, and T2o is clearly at the bottom of my range and not worth the investment of even a call against a capable opponent.

I hold T7o on the button (SB) with a stack of roughly 100BB. In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, T7o is below the median hand strength and is typically a fold in equilibrium charts, though sometimes mixed with limping. My opponent 'Flash' has defended their Big Blind by calling 3 out of 5 times against my raises so far (60% defense frequency), suggesting that an attempted steal with a weak hand is not automatically profitable. Since the hand plays poorly post-flop and the fold equity is not guaranteed to be high enough to justify a raise with trash, folding is the disciplined decision to conserve chips.

K9o is a solid hand to open from the small blind in heads-up play. Based on previous hands, Pro has shown a willingness to both call and fold to 3x raises. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot with a hand that has good high-card value.

I hold Qh 5s in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. My opponent has shown a tendency to raise 3x with decent frequency but has also folded several buttons, suggesting a range that isn't purely 100% ATC (Any Two Cards). While Q5o has raw equity around 40% against a standard opening range, the pot odds require 33%, but the realization of that equity is difficult Out of Position with an unsuited, disconnected hand. Against a 3x sizing specifically, Q5o falls into the folding range as it suffers from reverse implied odds and poor playability. I fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 4c 2d, which is effectively the absolute bottom of my range. Despite having position post-flop in heads-up play, this hand has very poor equity and playability. While it is suited, its playability is extremely low, and given the opponent's low fold-to-steal frequency, a raise here is unlikely to be profitable immediately. Limping is weak and exposes me to isolation raises or difficult post-flop spots out of position if checked. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the optimal strategy here.

I am on the button with Ace-Trey offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in a heads-up game. I have a significant chip lead over Pro (13,500 vs 6,350). Recently, Pro has been folding to many of my pre-flop raises. I will continue my aggressive strategy of opening for 3x the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s5s, facing a 3x raise to 300 from the Button (Flash). Flash has been opening a very wide range of hands from the button. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), requiring roughly 33% raw equity. Suited connectors like 54s have approximately 36-38% equity against a standard wide button opening range. In Hand #102, I defended with 5c4c in an identical spot. Despite being out of position, the hand's playability and the stack depth (63BB) justify a call to realize my equity and potential implied odds.

I hold 4s5s on a board of 9s8s2h, giving me a flush draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor (Flash). Flash has shown a tendency to c-bet frequently. Checking allows me to see his action; if he bets, I can decide between calling to realize my equity or check-raising as a semi-bluff. Leading out (donk betting) is generally less optimal here as it denies Flash the opportunity to bluff with his air or continuation bet light.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-high and the opponent called from the big blind. The flop is 9s-8s-2h, which doesn't directly connect with my hand but gives me two overcards to the board. Pro has checked to me. While this texture is better for the big blind's calling range, I have a range advantage and the initiative. A continuation bet here can often take down the pot immediately, as Pro has been playing relatively fit-or-fold on flops lately. I will place a standard-sized continuation bet of half the pot to test their strength.

I hold a flush draw (4s5s) on a 9s8s2h board. Facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600), I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring 25% equity to call. My flush draw alone gives me approximately 36% equity over two streets, making this a mathematically profitable call. Raising (check-raising) is an option but risky with a low flush draw, as I could be dominated by higher flush draws or face a 3-bet that forces me to fold my equity. Calling keeps the opponent's bluffs in the hand and controls the pot size while out of position.

I hold 4s5s on a board of 9s8s2h8h. I turned a flush draw on the flop and the board has now paired with the Eight on the turn. As the out-of-position player who called the flop bet, the standard line is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) on a paired board is generally inadvisable as the Eight interacts well with both ranges, and I have no showdown value (5-high). Checking gives me the option to see a free river if the opponent checks back, or to evaluate the price if he bets again. My plan is to check and react to his action.

The turn brings the 8h, pairing the board. After Pro called my flop bet, their range likely contains 9s, 8s, small pocket pairs, or spade draws. While Ace-high has some showdown value against missed draws, betting into a paired board where the opponent has already shown interest is risky. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown or hit an Ace on the river.

I hold 4s5s (5-high) on a board of 9s-8s-2h-8h-6h. The river completes a backdoor heart flush and pairs the board with eights, while I have missed my flop flush draw and have zero showdown value. However, the opponent's check-back on the turn is highly indicative of weakness or a capped range (likely Ace-high, a weak pair, or a weak 9), as strong hands like trips or draws typically barrel that turn card. The river 6h is a 'scare card' that completes flushes and connects straights, favoring my range as the Big Blind defender who could have backed into a flush or held an 8. If I check, I will almost certainly lose to Ace-high or weak pairs checking back. Leading out with a bluff is the only way to win the pot. A bet of roughly half-pot is sufficient to fold out his air (missed spade draws, overcards) and put weak pairs in a tough spot. I will bet 625 to steal the pot.

On the river, the board is 9s-8s-2h-8h-6h. I have Ah-3d, which is only Ace-high. I raised pre-flop and followed up with a continuation bet on the flop, which Pro called. The turn was an 8h, pairing the board, and we both checked. On the river, Pro has led out for 625 into a 1200 pot. Pro's line (call pre, call flop, check turn, lead river) is very indicative of a hand that has showdown value, likely a 9 or an 8. Since Pro has been described as playing 'fit-or-fold,' this lead suggests they have connected with the board. My Ace-high has almost no chance of being the best hand here against a value-oriented lead, and there is no need to risk chips in this spot given my massive stack advantage. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold 7h 5c in the Small Blind. This is a weak off-suit hand (bottom 20% of range). I previously folded a similar hand (7s 5c) in Hand #105. With a stack depth of 70BB, there is no pressure to play marginal or junk hands out of position or to attempt a steal with low equity. The standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with Q4o. While not a powerhouse hand, in heads-up play, Q-high hands are often worth playing for a minimum raise or a call to see a flop. I hold Kd Js in the Big Blind. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Flash has been raising to 300 consistently with a very wide range of hands. While a 3-bet for value is an option, calling allows me to keep their range wide (including dominated hands) and play a pot in position relative to the betting action (though technically OOP post-flop, I close the action pre-flop). I will call to realize my equity and evaluate the flop.

I hold KdJs on a Jd4sQc flop. I have middle pair with the second-best kicker. This is a decent hand heads-up, but I am out of position. The board connects with many broadway hands in the opponent's raising range (AQ, KQ, KT, T9). Leading out doesn't gain much value from worse hands and folds out bluffs. Checking allows me to induce bluffs or call a continuation bet from a wide range. My hand is strong enough to call a bet, but not strong enough to build a large pot immediately out of position. Therefore, checking is the standard and most prudent play.

I have flopped top and bottom pair (Queens and Fours) on a board of Q-J-4. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. I need to bet for value while protecting against potential straight draws like T-9 or K-T. A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a standard continuation bet that can get called by a wide range of hands including Jacks, weaker Queens, or draws.

I hold Middle Pair with a strong kicker (KdJs) on a Queen-high board (Qc Jd 4s). My opponent, Flash, has been raising nearly 100% of buttons and follows up with c-bets frequently using a standard half-pot sizing (300). Against such a wide range, my hand is well ahead of their air and draws (like KT, T9, straight draws) and dominates worse Jacks. While I am behind a Queen or better, folding here would be too tight against an aggressive opponent. Raising would likely only get action from better hands (Qx, sets, two pair) and fold out the bluffs I beat. Calling is the best option to keep the pot manageable, keep their bluffing range in, and re-evaluate on the turn. I need 25% equity to call, which I easily possess.

I hold KdJs on a board of Jd 4s Qc 6d. I have second pair with a King kicker and the King of Diamonds giving me a high flush draw blocker/draw. The 6d is mostly a blank but brings a flush draw. I am out of position against an aggressive opponent who opens wide and c-bets frequently. Leading (donk betting) here serves little purpose as it folds out his bluffs and isolates me against Queens or better. Checking allows me to pot control, keep his bluffing range wide, and potentially catch a bluff or see a free river. I plan to check-call a reasonable bet given his wide range, or check-down if allowed.

I have top and bottom pair (Queens and Fours) on a relatively draw-heavy board (Q-J-4-6). Pro called pre-flop and called a continuation bet on the flop, which indicates they likely have a piece of the board, such as a Jack, a weaker Queen, or a straight draw like K-T or T-9. The 6 of diamonds on the turn is a blank for most of that range. I need to continue betting for value and to charge any draws. A bet of 600 (half the pot) is large enough to extract value from Jacks and draws while keeping the pot manageable if Pro has a hand like Q-J.

I hold KdJs (second pair, strong kicker) on a Jd4sQc6d board. My opponent, who plays aggressively and opens wide, has fired a second barrel of 600 into a 1200 pot (half-pot). This sizing is consistent with their previous patterns for both value and bluffs. I am beating all straight draws (KT, T9) and flush draws, which make up a significant portion of their barreling range. Getting 3:1 pot odds (requiring 25% equity), my hand is too strong to fold heads-up. Raising would only isolate me against better hands, so calling is the most profitable line to realize equity and bluff-catch on the river.

I have improved to Two Pair (Kings and Jacks) on the river, but the King is a dangerous card as it completes T9 and AT straights and improves KQ to a better two pair. Being out of position against an aggressive opponent who has double-barreled, leading out is risky as it likely isolates me against a stronger range. Checking allows me to induce bluffs from missed draws or potentially call a reasonable bet from worse value hands (like QJ or AK).

I have two pair (Queens and Fours). The board now contains a King, which is a significant card. It completes a straight for T9 and improves hands like KQ, KJ, or KT to a better two-pair or higher. Pro has called two streets of value, suggesting they have a piece of this board. While my two pair is strong in heads-up play, the river card makes it difficult to get value from worse hands like a Jack or a weaker Queen, and I am likely only getting called or raised by hands that beat me. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value without risking a check-raise.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the Button (Small Blind) is a powerful position. Holding Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o), I have an Ace, which is statistically strong heads-up. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop if the Big Blind folds, and guarantees I play the hand in position post-flop if called. A min-raise to 200 is the most efficient sizing to accomplish these goals.

I have middle pair (fives) on an 8-5-4 board with a flush draw possible. This is a decent hand with some showdown value, but it is not a powerhouse. I hold A2o on a 5h-4c-8h flop. I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw to the wheel (need a 3). On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds is an overcard that likely doesn't help my pair of fives. Given that the pre-flop aggressor checked back on the flop, my middle pair still has some showdown value. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm likely only called by better hands or draws, and I want to reach showdown as cheaply as possible.

I hold Ace-high (Ah 2s) on a board of 5h-4c-8h-Qd. After checking back the flop, the pot remains small (400). The Queen on the turn is a high card that connects somewhat with my perceived range (broadways), but since I checked the flop, I haven't represented strength aggressively. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff and risks getting raised or called by a pair (8x, 5x, 4x, or a Q). Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see the river, and potentially win at showdown or improve to a straight/top pair. Pot control is preferred here.

On the river, I still have my pair of fives. The board hasn't changed much except for the deuce, which is unlikely to have helped Pro's range given the previous action. Both Pro and I checked on the flop and turn, which suggests that neither of us has a particularly strong hand. My pair of fives has significant showdown value in this small pot. By checking, I allow the hand to go to showdown for free, winning against Ace-high or other missed hands, while avoiding the risk of being raised by a better hand that Pro might have been slow-playing or a marginal hand that improved to a pair of eights or queens.

On the River, the board is [5h 4c 8h Qd 2c]. I have improved to a pair of Deuces with an Ace kicker. The pot is small (400), and the action has been checked through on the flop and turn. Betting here is unlikely to get called by a worse hand (Ace-high or King-high usually fold), and unlikely to fold out a better hand (pairs usually call a small bet in this spot). I am on the button with K3o. In heads-up play, any King is typically an opening hand from the small blind to exert pressure on the big blind. I am in the Big Blind with Q7o facing a standard 3x raise from the Button. In heads-up play, the Button opens a very wide range of hands. I am out of position on an Ace-high board (Ah 6d 3h) with Queen-high and no immediate draws. The board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes many Ax combinations. I have bottom pair with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. After Pro checked to me, checking back is a solid play to control the size of the pot and realize my equity. I hold Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (a 4 gives me the nut straight). The opponent checked back the flop on an Ace-high board, which often caps their range (likely no strong Ace). I have bottom pair (threes) with a King kicker on a board of A-6-3-5. After checking back on the flop to control the pot, Pro has checked again on the turn. The opponent raised pre-flop and then checked back both the flop (Ace-high) and the turn, which indicates a capped range likely consisting of weak pairs, underpairs, or missed high cards. Bluffing into this line is risky as he might look me up with any pair given the checked-down action. Checking is the prudent play to preserve chips; if he checks back, I likely lose, but I avoid throwing chips away with a low-probability bluff. If he bets, I can easily fold.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on a board of A-6-3-5-J. Pro has checked to me on every street. Given the action, my pair of 3s has significant showdown value. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and most hands that would call me likely have me beat (an Ace, a 6, a 5, or a Jack). I hold Ace-Ten offsuit on the Button (SB), which is a strong hand in heads-up play, well within the top range of opening hands. I am on the button with 9s8d, which is a solid hand to play in a heads-up situation. The pot is small (150) and I have a significant stack lead. I hold King-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well within the top percentage of hands. I am out of position on an Ace-high flop (Ac 9c 8h) holding King-Jack offsuit. The Ace heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. On the button, I raised pre-flop with 9s8d and have now flopped middle two pair (Nines and Eights) on an Ac-9c-8h board. On the flop of Ac 9c 8h, I hold Ks Jc (King-high). My opponent has bet half-pot (300 into 600). The Ace heavily favors the opponent's pre-flop raising range. I hold a pocket pair of threes (3c, 3h) on the Button (Small Blind). In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a statistically strong hand, significantly ahead of a random range. My stack is 73 BBs. I'm holding suited Jack-Five in the Big Blind. Pro opened for a min-raise (200), giving me 3:1 odds to call. Given the playability of suited cards and the favorable pot odds, a call is standard here to see a flop, despite being out of position.

I have Jack-high on a very dry board (King, Trey, Deuce) after calling a pre-flop raise. This board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Kings and Aces. I have flopped a set of Threes on a K-3-2 rainbow board, a very strong hand. The board texture is dry, meaning there are few draws. I want to start building the pot immediately for value. A standard c-bet size of 200 (half pot) is appropriate to keep his range wide while extracting value.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five of spades. The flop is King of hearts, Trey of spades, Deuce of diamonds, and the pot is 600. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 200 into the 400 pot. I am on the button with a suited Queen (Qd 6d). In heads-up play, this is a strong opening hand. I will raise to 300, which has been my standard open size from the button, to put pressure on the big blind and seize the initiative in the hand with position.

We are heads-up in the Big Blind with A8o facing a 3bb raise (300) from the Button. We have ~75bb effective stacks. A8o is a strong hand heads-up (top ~20%) and is well above the folding threshold. On a board of [Kh Th Kd], the texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Flash) who has a significant range advantage with more strong Kings and broadway hands. I hold Qd 6d on a Kh-Th-Kd board. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and some backdoor diamond potential. The board is [Kh Th Kd], which heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Flash) who has all the strong Kings and full houses in their range. As the Big Blind caller, my range is capped. I hold Queen-Ten offsuit (Qc Ts) on the Button. In a heads-up match, this is a strong starting hand (top ~28-30% of hands). I am holding K3o in the Big Blind. Pro (the button) has opened for a minimum raise to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot that will be 400. I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a paired board (7-K-7). The board texture is K-7-7 rainbow, which is very dry. As the pre-flop raiser, the King connects well with my range, but the paired 7s make it difficult for the opponent to have connected unless they hold a King or a 7. I have top pair with a King. Pro's check on the flop (K-7-7) suggests they likely do not have a King or a Seven, as those are hands they would typically continuation bet for value or protection on such a dry board. The Nine on the turn is relatively safe.
